figures.caption: Table 9: Score changes for interventions with p = 0.75. Bold traits indicate where SYS+SIT performs better than SYS alone by either eliminating a significant drift or flipping an undesirable drift into a desirable direction (i.e. significantly increasing a pro-social trait or significantly decreasing an anti-social one).

Figure 1: The steps of our approach: (1) assign different personas to the language models (LMs) using default, benevolent or malicious system prompts, (2) conduct pre-game persona assessment and identify core implicit traits, (3) agents compete in multi-turn social dilemma games, (4) post-game assessment quantifies effects of misalignment contagion and our steering with implicit traits (SIT) intervention.

Table 3: Anti-social trait score changes (post minus pre) under different game conditions. Bold traits are Uni and/or Mix game settings with significant increases. Bold SYS+SIT values indicate where SYS+SIT outperforms SYS. *p < 0.001.

Table 2: Pro-social trait score changes (post minus pre) under different game conditions. Bold traits are Uni and/or Mix game settings with significant decreases. Bold SYS+SIT values indicate where SYS+SIT outperforms SYS. *p < 0.001.

Table 10: Score changes for interventions with p = 0.50. Bold traits indicate where SYS+SIT performs better than SYS alone by either eliminating a significant drift or flipping an undesirable drift into a desirable direction (i.e. significantly increasing a pro-social trait or significantly decreasing an anti-social one).

Table 5: Percentage of intervention games where the default agent had various implicit traits, i.e. where the pre-game score is greater than θ = 0.85. Default agents tend to be initially pro-social, hence the absence of anti-social traits.

Table 11: Score changes for interventions with p = 0.25. Bold traits indicate where SYS+SIT performs better than SYS alone by either eliminating a significant drift or flipping an undesirable drift into a desirable direction (i.e. significantly increasing a pro-social trait or significantly decreasing an anti-social one).
Table 4: Number of games by game setting and intervention condition.
Table 7: Win rates for different models averages across games in the system repetition (SYS) intervention and steering with implicit traits (SYS+SIT) intervention.

Figure 2: Pre-game and post-game trait scores for DeepSeek-V3.2 players steered to assume three distinct personas: benevolent (green), default (blue) and malicious (red). Axes with PS and AS prefixes refer to pro-social and anti-social traits respectively. N0 and N1 refer to neutral risk-averse and risk-seeking traits respectively.

Figure 3: PCA analysis for Iterative Prisoner’s Dilemma. All players use Llama-3.3-70BInstruct as base model. Top figures use system prompt interventions (p = 1.0) and bottom figures use SIT inventions (p = 1.0). Player personas are: Default (blue), Benevolent (Green), Malicious (Red).

Table 8: Score changes for interventions with p = 1.0. Bold traits indicate where Uni and/or Mix game settings show significant changes in the direction of anti-sociality. Bold SYS+SIT values indicate where SYS+SIT outperforms SYS by eliminating significant changes in no intervention conditions or by shifting the agent in the direction of pro-sociality.
